children.name: Pica
children.description: The true magpies — unmistakable black-and-white birds with an extraordinarily long, iridescent green tail. Among the most intelligent animals on Earth, magpies pass the mirror self-recognition test, use tools, hold 'funerals' for dead companions, and cache food while watching for observers, then move the cache if they suspect they have been seen. The Eurasian Magpie has lived alongside humans for millennia and thrives in cities, gardens, and farmland across the entire Palearctic.

children.name: Corvus
children.description: Crows and ravens — the largest and most wide-ranging genus in the family, with an almost global distribution. Corvus species are renowned for problem-solving, social complexity, and long memory for individual human faces. The Common Raven is the largest passerine and one of the most intelligent birds known, capable of planning, deception, and playful behaviour.

children.name: Coloeus
children.description: Jackdaws — compact, sociable corvids with pale eyes and a distinctive silver-grey nape. Among the most social corvids, they form long-term pair bonds, nest colonially, and regularly follow larger ungulates and humans to catch disturbed insects. The Eurasian Jackdaw's silver eye gives it an unusually expressive, inquisitive gaze.

children.name: Garrulus
children.description: The Eurasian Jays — dazzlingly patterned corvids with a sky-blue wing panel, pinkish-brown body, and a raucous, scolding call that serves as a woodland alarm system. Jays are paramount seed dispersers: a single bird buries thousands of acorns each autumn in separate caches up to several kilometres from the source tree, and though it recovers most, enough forgotten caches germinate to drive oak forest regeneration across Europe.

children.name: Cyanopica
children.description: Azure-winged magpies — slender, long-tailed corvids with pale underparts and delicate sky-blue wings and tail. Unusually social for corvids, they breed cooperatively with helpers at the nest. They have a striking disjunct distribution: the Iberian Magpie in Portugal and Spain, and the Azure-winged Magpie from the Russian Far East to Japan and China — a distribution once attributed to human introduction but now confirmed as natural.

children.name: Nucifraga
children.description: Nutcrackers — thickset corvids of mountain conifer forests, with a specialised sublingual pouch that can carry up to 90 pine seeds at once. The Spotted Nutcracker is the primary disperser of Swiss stone pine and Arolla pine across the Alps and Carpathians; without it, these trees could not colonise new ground above the existing treeline. The mutualism is so tight that the trees produce seeds too large for most birds other than nutcrackers to handle.

children.name: Pyrrhocorax
children.description: Choughs — sleek, all-black corvids with curved bills and buoyant, acrobatic flight that makes them the most aerial members of the family. They inhabit mountain cliffs, sea-cliffs, and upland pastures, probing the soil and dung for invertebrates with their specialised bills. Their high, ringing calls echo off cliff faces throughout their range from Ireland to China.

children.name: Perisoreus
children.description: Siberian jays — small, fluffy corvids of the taiga, with soft grey-brown plumage and a rusty tail. They are year-round residents of boreal forest and have developed an extraordinary relationship with human hunters, following them through the forest to scavenge scraps — a behaviour that has made them symbols of the northern wilderness in Scandinavian folklore. They cache food year-round and retrieve caches with remarkable spatial memory during winter.

children.name: Urocissa
children.description: Blue magpies — spectacular corvids of South and East Asian forests, with vivid cobalt-blue plumage, a white-tipped tail sometimes longer than the body, and a red or yellow bill. They are noisy, gregarious birds of forest edges and hillsides, often moving in small parties. Despite their dazzling appearance they behave much like other corvids — omnivorous, opportunistic, and bold.

children.name: Dendrocitta
children.description: Treepies — long-tailed corvids of South and Southeast Asian woodlands with warm rufous and grey plumage. Unlike most corvids they are strongly arboreal, racing through canopy and clambering along branches in search of fruit, insects, eggs, and small vertebrates. Their loud, varied calls are a characteristic sound of the Indian subcontinent's forests and gardens.
